216007.explanation: Velsen Lokaal is not against tax relief, but at this moment the property tax revenue is urgently needed to maintain the current level of amenities.

216008.explanation: Velsen Lokaal supports abolishing the dog tax, provided we can find coverage to compensate for the lost revenue.

216009.explanation: Velsen Lokaal refers here to City Marketing and the connecting role it plays in the region. Velsen is a vibrant municipality we are proud of. More economic activity contributes to a stronger position in the region.

216010.explanation: Of course, Velsen Lokaal wants to stimulate the generation of sustainable energy. Subsidies already exist from the national government and the province. Therefore, Velsen Lokaal does not see it as a task of the municipality to invest money in this.

216011.explanation: We are in favor of spreading status holders across the municipality. This means status holders should be placed as little as possible in IJmuiden and Velsen-Noord. At this moment, urgency is needed to be able to spread. As soon as this is no longer necessary, the urgency for status holders disappears in our view.

216012.explanation: Velsen Lokaal finds it important that newcomers find a place in our community and integrate as well as possible. The (extra) funding for this is, in Velsen Lokaal’s opinion, a task for the national government.

216013.explanation: This is a decision already made by the municipal council, and Velsen Lokaal wants to adhere to it. Velsen has a task and responsibility, and in this way we give shape to it and spare the more vulnerable neighborhoods.

216016.explanation: Within clear agreements, Velsen Lokaal supports giving residents more ownership of their own environment and thus also for green maintenance (e.g., via Samen Velsen). However, it must remain workable for the municipal green service to avoid duplicated work or wasting money.

216017.explanation: Velsen Lokaal believes that everyone should be able to participate in sports. Membership of a sports club helps counter isolation and loneliness. There are already schemes for people with limited means to reduce membership fees; we want to continue these. Full payment is not necessary.

216020.explanation: More enforcement officers mean higher costs. If it becomes truly necessary, expansion is open for discussion, but for now we assume the current staffing. Velsen Lokaal sees that the enforcement officers do excellent work and are an important link in the municipality’s safety and livability.

216021.explanation: Velsen Lokaal is not in favor of more camera surveillance by default due to privacy concerns. If necessary in certain situations, we are not opposed to it.

216025.explanation: This is in line with the housing vision established by the council. IJmuiden and Velsen-Noord already have many social rental homes. To balance the housing mix in the neighborhoods more, we foresee new social housing only in the rest of the municipality.

216026.explanation: This is in line with the housing vision established by the council. IJmuiden and Velsen-Noord already have many social rental homes. To balance the housing mix in the neighborhoods more, we foresee new social housing only in the rest of the municipality.

216027.explanation: For Velsen Lokaal, this is the starting point. We can imagine there are exceptions, which should be assessed on a case-by-case basis.

216028.explanation: Although Velsen hardly has vacant office buildings and this is in principle not a municipal task, Velsen Lokaal wants to seize every opportunity to create as much living space as possible.

216030.explanation: No one should have to live in poverty. Within the current poverty vision, the right attention is given to this, and Velsen Lokaal wants to maintain that.

216031.explanation: Work is an important way for Velsen Lokaal to participate in society. If someone is not disabled, we encourage everyone to keep striving for paid employment. Informal caregiving is very important but is separate from employment status.

216032.explanation: Velsen Lokaal sees the positive effects of youth work in the municipality. By investing in our youth, we contribute to limiting problems among this group and invest in a bright future.

216033.explanation: At the moment, parking pressure is too high in large parts of Velsen. Velsen Lokaal believes something must be done. At the same time, our residents want more green and space in the neighborhoods. That means we have to look for smart parking solutions.

216034.explanation: Velsen Lokaal wants to tackle parking pressure and is open to smart solutions that also enable housing construction. Permit parking can help with this but is not a goal in itself for us. We always look for what best fits the neighborhood.

216035.explanation: Velsen Lokaal believes that on roads with buildings alongside or without separated bike paths, the maximum speed may be reduced to 30 km/h to create a safer road. Through roads (where buses run) should remain 50 km/h in our view.

216036.explanation: Velsen Lokaal believes that every neighborhood should have a place where people can come together and meet each other. Meeting locally is essential. How this place looks will differ per neighborhood.

216038.explanation: Velsen Lokaal wants everyone to have the opportunity to think and talk along. Young people often experience a barrier. By deploying a youth council, we expect that young people will become more involved in their municipality and living environment.

216039.explanation: Velsen Lokaal considers good and affordable public transport important and is positive about solutions that promote this. A travel allowance can help, but structurally free travel is financially unfeasible. We see this primarily as a task for the national government.

216040.explanation: Velsen Lokaal supports clean air. Accelerated closure of parts can only occur if a new installation is available and this does not jeopardize continuity of operations and employment.

216044.explanation: Nursing homes unfortunately no longer exist. Therefore, Velsen Lokaal is committed to good support for elderly people living at home. We ensure they receive the help they need so everyone can age in Velsen in a pleasant and appropriate way.

216045.explanation: Trees contribute to better livability. Velsen Lokaal believes green should be accessible to everyone and promotes the 3-30-300 approach: everyone should be able to see three trees from home, 30% of the neighborhood should be leafy greenery, and within 300 meters there should be a green area.

216046.explanation: For people on a low income, the municipality offers an affordable health insurance, ensuring everyone has access to healthcare facilities. Velsen Lokaal supports this. Couples therapy is separate from this.

216048.explanation: Velsen Lokaal appreciates that this approach allows everyone to 'discover' the library in an accessible way. Unfortunately, reading and literacy are not yet a given for everyone.

216049.explanation: Velsen Lokaal believes it is important that young informal carers can take occasional breaks. Replacement care is often well arranged through volunteer organizations. The municipality supports finding appropriate help without leading to high additional costs.

216051.explanation: Velsen Lokaal wants to seize every opportunity to create as much living space as possible. As far as we are concerned, this should be limited to vacant retail premises outside the central shopping areas. Within shopping areas, we want to encourage entrepreneurs to establish attractive shops.

216052.explanation: Sustainably upgrading a home is the responsibility of the homeowner. In some cases, subsidies are available.

216053.explanation: Although there is already a bus stop within a 5-minute walk, Velsen Lokaal does see opportunities to advocate for, for example, a summer line or extra route to also make the IJmuiderslag accessible by bus. In that case, an additional stop near the swimming pool can also be realized.

216054.explanation: In this respect, Velsen Lokaal believes the municipality does not need to adopt policy that deviates from the national government’s policy.

216055.explanation: Velsen Lokaal finds it important to properly capture rainwater. In new construction, this is already done with wadi's: green pits where rainwater slowly seeps into the ground. This helps prevent water nuisance. We would like to see more of such places in Velsen.

216056.explanation: This measure only makes sense in places where it actually resolves an issue. Velsen Lokaal is open to considering this measure if it is necessary and clearly contributes to solving a problem.

216057.explanation: The current collection method is the fairest according to Velsen Lokaal. The more residual waste you produce, the more you pay. We also see this as an incentive to separate waste even better.

216058.explanation: The regulations are currently clear, and various exceptions cause extra hassle and increase the chance of errors, which results in extra nuisance for people. Moreover, extra actions in the process require additional effort and thus higher costs.

216059.explanation: The four-week period encourages young people to first seek work or education themselves before applying for benefits. The idea is that young people have opportunities and benefits are a last safety net. It is an activating measure, not a punishment.

216061.explanation: Velsen has much to offer entrepreneurs. Velsen Lokaal does not hear signals of companies staying away because of permit costs. We remain alert and open to improvement if needed. Entrepreneurship should be encouraged.

216062.explanation: If companies want to improve their terrain together, that is their own responsibility. The municipality is happy to facilitate this but financing is the companies’ responsibility.

216063.explanation: Velsen Lokaal supports an inclusive society where everyone can practice the sport they want.

216064.explanation: Language is crucial for integration. Velsen Lokaal wants status holders to start working quickly because that is the best way to learn language and culture. Language lessons remain important to fully participate, but extra funding is not needed. It is about active participation in society.

216007.explanation: Raising or lowering the property tax (OZB) is not a goal in itself. To keep our services in order and realize ambitions, money is needed. However, we believe we must manage with the money we currently ask for and do not want to increase the OZB further at this moment.

216008.explanation: D66Velsen has ensured that the dog tax remains stable and does not increase arbitrarily anymore. Abolishing it now is too far, as much of our public space and parks are specifically designed for dogs. Owners can reasonably contribute a bit to that.

216009.explanation: D66Velsen aims for the greenest industrial zone in Europe with a strong offshore industry. To achieve this, we must also attract the right companies here. Investing in this as a municipality is justified.

216010.explanation: D66Velsen wants to support initiatives to generate sustainable energy together. Much more sustainable energy is needed, and local production of energy is an important way to counter grid congestion. This keeps the energy transition and sustainability feasible and affordable for more people.

216011.explanation: D66Velsen wants everyone to have a roof over their head, including status holders. Moreover, we want to prevent that they most often end up in neighborhoods with the shortest registration duration, such as IJmuiden and Velsen-Noord. Urgency is the only way to steer on this, which also benefits integration and livability.

216012.explanation: D66Velsen believes it is important that newcomers get a good start in the Netherlands as quickly as possible. This helps people integrate faster, and we believe they also find work much sooner. An investment upfront therefore pays for itself quickly.

216013.explanation: D66Velsen wants to make a sustainable contribution to solving the housing crisis by working on an asylum seekers' reception location. The goal is not for it to be in Santpoort-Zuid per se, but after extensive research the De Elta site in Santpoort-Zuid has proven to be the most suitable location.

216016.explanation: D66Velsen wholeheartedly supports residents who want to maintain public green spaces in their own neighborhoods. This often makes the area more beautiful and cheaper to maintain. We are willing to provide compensation for this.

216017.explanation: D66Velsen believes everyone should be able to participate. However, the maze of benefits that differs considerably per municipality causes people on social assistance to view these arrangements negatively. We want to move towards a fixed package for everyone below 120% of the social assistance norm.

216020.explanation: D66Velsen sees safety primarily as the police's responsibility. Municipal enforcement officers are also an important link, for example in tackling youth nuisance. However, a lot has already been invested in this. Further expansion is not a priority for D66Velsen. We prefer to invest that money in prevention.

216021.explanation: D66Velsen supports targeted, temporary camera surveillance only where it is absolutely and demonstrably necessary. We do not favor structural expansion of (non-targeted) camera surveillance in public spaces.

216025.explanation: D66Velsen observes that these neighborhoods have relatively few social rental homes, making it almost impossible for young starters from these villages to begin. Extra social rental homes are therefore urgently needed in these neighborhoods.

216026.explanation: D66Velsen sees the housing shortage is so great that you have no choice. Moreover, these neighborhoods still have a huge shortage of social rental homes for elderly people to move into, or starter homes. D66Velsen therefore specifically wants to build social rental homes for young people and the elderly.

216027.explanation: D66Velsen sees that especially affordable housing shortages are so large that it is incomprehensible people buy homes as an investment. We therefore want people to be allowed to buy homes only if they intend to live in them themselves.

216028.explanation: D66Velsen currently sees few vacant office buildings outside intensive industrial zones, but where this does occur we are willing to cooperate in transforming them into homes.

216030.explanation: D66Velsen stands for equal opportunities. We see poverty as one of the main reasons people cannot participate in society. We would therefore rather invest more in poverty reduction than cut its funding.

216031.explanation: D66Velsen believes that people providing intensive informal care should not have an obligation to apply for jobs if they live on social assistance benefits. This obviously applies only to intensive caregiving, not, for example, when you do weekly groceries for someone.

216032.explanation: D66Velsen sees that youth work very effectively helps to reduce nuisance and guide young people in a positive way. That makes it an investment in good youth and a better future. It should not be cut.

216033.explanation: For D66Velsen, the highest goal is a livable neighborhood. For most neighborhoods, that means investing better in more green spaces and trees instead of more cars in the street. There are smarter ways to solve parking pressure than creating more parking spaces.

216034.explanation: D66Velsen sees that parking pressure in some neighborhoods is so high that permits are the only solution. The goal is the livability of the neighborhood, not filling the municipal coffers. The first permit should always be very cheap, as we understand many people need a car.

216035.explanation: D66Velsen wants to make 30 km/h the standard speed limit within built-up areas. Of course, there are exceptions for main roads around villages. Furthermore, it should be prevented that attractive cut-through routes arise through residential neighborhoods.

216036.explanation: D66Velsen wants every neighborhood to have a place that serves as a kind of second living room for everyone. An accessible space where nothing is expected of you, but where you can meet others.

216038.explanation: D66Velsen wants to involve young people more in the political decisions that need to be made. This can be done in various ways, but a youth council is certainly one possibility.

216039.explanation: D66Velsen believes everyone should be able to participate. However, the maze of benefits that vary greatly per municipality causes people on social assistance to view arrangements negatively. We want to move towards one fixed package for everyone below 120% of the social assistance norm.

216040.explanation: D66Velsen puts health first, and that means shutting down the most outdated installations of Tata Steel as quickly as possible. However, we also focus on becoming the greenest industrial zone in Europe. Tata is an important player in this, so the pace must be realistic for a green alternative.

216044.explanation: D66Velsen wants to stimulate moving onward and upward, which means more homes and living concepts for the elderly. We are willing to contribute by facilitating capacity and charging a lower land price.

216045.explanation: D66Velsen has the Velsens Greening Offensive as one of the five breakthroughs in our program. Good living is green living. More greening in neighborhoods is necessary to combat, for example, water nuisance and heat stress. This is one of D66Velsen’s priorities for this term.

216046.explanation: D66Velsen is willing to reimburse couples therapy for families with children. This prevents children from becoming the victims, much misery later on, and high other social costs. However, it goes too far for us to offer this to everyone as a municipality.

216048.explanation: D66Velsen has a free library subscription for everyone as one of the five breakthroughs in its program. The library is much more than just a place to borrow books. We believe everyone should be able to learn lifelong; the library is where this starts.

216049.explanation: D66Velsen sees that some informal caregivers fill their days only with caregiving. This can be beautiful, but also very exhausting. It is important to regularly provide replacement care for these people to prevent burnout.

216051.explanation: D66Velsen is willing to cooperate in transforming shops into homes when they are located outside core shopping areas. We want to keep those areas specifically for shops to maintain our amenities.

216052.explanation: D66Velsen considers reducing gas consumption very important. This helps sustainability and household budgets. Municipalities also receive funding from the national government for this. D66Velsen has additionally ensured that money earmarked for energy poverty continues to be spent on this.

216053.explanation: D66Velsen considers a bus stop at swimming pool De Heerenduinen a good idea. Possibly by having, for example, bus line 3 follow a different route.

216054.explanation: D66Velsen believes paid advertising in public spaces should have limits. Smoking, alcohol, and gambling should not be promoted in public spaces. This also fits within our focus on prevention.

216055.explanation: D66Velsen is fully committed to greening through the Velsens Greening Offensive. This is the solution for rainwater drainage. We also want to add water in neighborhoods where possible. This helps with rainwater drainage and cooling.

216056.explanation: D66Velsen is not against paid parking but sees no additional locations that we consider logical for paid parking. In shopping areas, we prefer to use blue zones. These usually work just as well to achieve the intended goal, namely turnover of parking spaces.

216057.explanation: D66Velsen focuses on a circular economy. This is only possible if we separate waste better and burn less. Since introducing the recycling rate, residual waste in Velsen has halved, a huge success. However, we must crack down harder on people placing bags next to bins.

216058.explanation: D66Velsen sees that people sometimes keep two homes to avoid deductions in their benefits. In times of housing shortage, we must find ways to counter this. A six-month trial living period could help.

216059.explanation: D66Velsen does not consider a four-week waiting period long. Moreover, this period is used to look with young people at whether another education or job sector is possible. First looking at what you can do is especially important under age 27.

216061.explanation: D66Velsen receives few complaints about fees being too high. Moreover, these fees must be cost-covering by law. Entrepreneurs also benefit more from quick processing of their applications than a small discount on fees. We prefer to focus on that.

216062.explanation: D66Velsen is willing to invest in public spaces in industrial areas where businesses strive for greening and sustainability. Together we prepare industrial zones for the future as the greenest industrial zone in Europe.

216063.explanation: D66Velsen believes all sports and play facilities must be accessible to people with disabilities. And some sports accommodations should have additional arrangements so people with specific disabilities can also participate in sports there.

216064.explanation: D66Velsen finds it important that refugees can participate in the Netherlands as quickly as possible. Investing in language lessons helps people find work sooner. This saves societal costs.

216007.explanation: Residents and entrepreneurs already pay enough. We choose low local taxes: property tax and levies are kept as low as possible, with a healthy budget and focus on core tasks.

216008.explanation: We want to abolish the dog tax. As long as that is not yet possible, we lower this targeted tax by limiting administrative costs so that dog owners pay less.

216009.explanation: We promote Velsen, the port, parks, the beach, and our villages with one recognizable story, together with entrepreneurs. This attracts businesses and visitors, creates jobs, and strengthens hospitality and shopping centers that residents benefit from daily.

216010.explanation: We welcome local initiatives for sustainable energy. The role of the municipality is mainly to facilitate and provide space. Financial support is not automatic; initiatives must primarily succeed on their own and through existing schemes.

216011.explanation: We provide temporary housing for migrants with a residence permit, but abolish their priority position for social rental housing.

216012.explanation: Integration focuses on learning Dutch and moving to education or work as quickly as possible, without allocating new funds.

216013.explanation: Velsen contributes its fair share to orderly, safe, and accountable reception; preferably in the outskirts (such as Spaarnwoude). Temporary and measurable, coordinated regionally and not at the expense of, for example, scarce neighborhood green spaces.

216016.explanation: The VVD values doers. Residents who maintain the greenery in their neighborhood take responsibility and deserve support. This increases involvement and can make maintenance more efficient and save costs for the municipality.

216017.explanation: Sports are important for health and participation. The municipality can lower barriers and stimulate participation, but fully paying for sports memberships for adults on social assistance is not a structural task of the municipality. There are already arrangements for children in these families.

216020.explanation: Safe streets are not a luxury. The VVD wants more visible enforcement officers in centers so that intimidation, nuisance, and vandalism are addressed immediately and residents feel safe.

216021.explanation: The VVD supports more camera surveillance where it demonstrably contributes to safety. We use this tool only with a concrete reason, in defined areas, and temporarily, so it is effective and does not infringe on freedom unnecessarily.

216025.explanation: The VVD wants livable, mixed neighborhoods in all centers of Velsen. This requires balanced housing development, not concentrating social rental housing mainly in the green village centers. We build where it fits, with attention to livability, capacity, and the character of each center and neighborhood.

216026.explanation: The VVD wants livable, mixed neighborhoods in all centers of Velsen. This requires balanced housing development, not concentrating social rental housing mainly in the green village centers. We build where it fits, with attention to livability, capacity, and the character of each center and neighborhood.

216027.explanation: The VVD wants the housing market in Velsen to work again. A self-occupancy obligation inhibits investments and blocks rental housing. By allowing private rentals, we increase the supply for starters and workers.

216028.explanation: The VVD wants faster building by speeding up procedures and improving turnover. Converting vacant offices is often expensive, slow, and limited in scale. The municipality should mainly provide space for feasible housing projects, not steer costly transformations.

216030.explanation: The VVD stands for sustainably addressing poverty. Help where needed, with emphasis on progression to work and without incentives that discourage extra work.

216031.explanation: Informal care deserves appreciation and support. At the same time, the VVD believes working must pay off and that the focus remains on moving people into work. Therefore, we opt for custom solutions and temporary leniency where needed, but not for a general exemption from the job application requirement.

216032.explanation: Youth work helps young people develop and stay away from nuisance and crime. Together with youth enforcement officers, this must visibly contribute to safety. We focus on effectiveness, not automatic cuts or expansions.

216033.explanation: Parking must be well arranged on the street. Where possible, we create extra parking spaces without compromising livability. This prevents parking pressure from shifting to surrounding streets.

216034.explanation: Parking in Velsen must remain practical and affordable. The VVD opposes introducing parking permits in neighborhoods because this often shifts parking pressure rather than solves it. We choose smart design and sufficient spaces, not extra burdens for residents.

216035.explanation: Not every street is the same. Where necessary, we make streets safe with good lighting and design that naturally leads to slower driving. A fixed 30 km/h rule for all roads does not fit with this.

216036.explanation: Velsen has many neighborhoods. A mandatory social and cultural center in every neighborhood is not realistic. We support meeting places where needed and prefer to make the best use of existing facilities rather than building new community centers everywhere.

216038.explanation: Involving young people in policy is important. This does not necessarily have to be through a fixed youth council. We prefer forms of participation that resonate with young people and reach more youth.

216039.explanation: Participation is important, also for people with a low income. The municipality helps where necessary but does not automatically opt for free bus transport. Targeted support works better than one fixed scheme.

216040.explanation: Emissions at Tata Steel must be reduced, and sustainability is essential. This must be done carefully and balanced with the retention of jobs and industry. We choose to implement agreed steps, not to apply extra pressure through accelerated closure.

216044.explanation: Good housing with care nearby is important to enable older people to live independently longer. The municipality's role is to enable this and take the lead, not to routinely co-finance residential care projects.

216045.explanation: More trees make neighborhoods more livable. We support this as long as greening is done carefully and is part of balanced and smarter design of public spaces, which also includes other functions like sufficient parking spaces.

216046.explanation: Relationship problems are impactful, even for people with little financial means. At the same time, therapy is not a core task of the municipality. Support for care and welfare goes through health insurance and professional help, not through municipal reimbursements.

216048.explanation: Reading and access to knowledge help people move forward and strengthen self-reliance. We want to lower barriers where needed but are critical of general ‘‘free for everyone’’ schemes. Targeted support works better and keeps facilities affordable.

216049.explanation: Young informal caregivers deserve attention and support. The municipality's role is mainly in signaling, guidance, and relieving burden where possible, not in structurally financing replacement care.

216051.explanation: The municipality does not do this itself, but by removing barriers to making vacant retail properties suitable for housing, more homes can be added. Converting can be a solution, provided it fits with the environment and the livability and vitality of shopping areas are preserved.

216052.explanation: Home insulation lowers energy bills and increases living comfort. Primarily, this is an investment by the owner. The municipality helps where necessary with targeted support and relief, but general and permanent subsidies are not a given.

216053.explanation: Good accessibility of sports facilities is important. An extra bus stop only makes sense if it fits into a logical and realistic bus route. This is currently not the case here. We prefer feasible solutions that actually work over advocacy without perspective.

216054.explanation: Addiction is a serious problem, but banning advertising in public spaces is patronizing and arbitrary. The municipality should inform and protect where necessary, not decide which legal products may or may not be visible.

216055.explanation: Good rainwater drainage prevents flooding and damage. The municipality must invest specifically in this, for example during maintenance and redesign of streets and squares. This is sensible and prevents higher costs later.

216056.explanation: Paid parking in Velsen is already targeted at busy spots, such as the center of IJmuiden and the beach. In other shopping areas, blue zones work well. We do not see further expansion of paid parking as necessary and it mainly affects residents and entrepreneurs.

216057.explanation: Those who produce more residual waste cause more costs. It is fairer that this is passed on so that waste separation pays off and total waste costs remain low. A fixed rate for everyone removes that incentive.

216058.explanation: We understand that cohabiting can be stressful if it affects a benefit. At the same time, the rules on this are nationally established. The municipality cannot deviate from this and must apply equal and explainable rules.

216059.explanation: The benefit is a safety net, not a starting point. The waiting period for young people encourages them to first seek work, education, or guidance. Abolishing it reduces this incentive and increases the chance of long-term dependency.

216061.explanation: Permits should not be more expensive than necessary. By working smarter and reducing regulations, the municipality can lower costs. This helps entrepreneurs without shifting burdens onto others.

216062.explanation: If companies invest together in their site, the municipality may support that. Through cooperation such as a business improvement district (BIZ), we ensure clean, safe business parks, less nuisance, and an attractive business climate that creates jobs and investments.

216063.explanation: Sport is for everyone. Therefore, we find it important that sports facilities are accessible to people with disabilities. We approach this practically and step by step, for example in maintenance and renewal, together with clubs.

216064.explanation: Learning Dutch is essential to participate and enter the workforce. We expect people who are allowed to stay here to learn Dutch quickly. The focus is on effective language lessons and results, not automatically on more money.

216007.explanation: GroenLinks believes that residents with higher incomes can also contribute a bit more. That is why we do not want to lower the property tax (ozb), but rather increase it slightly each year, up to a maximum of 10 euros extra per year for an average homeowner. This will raise 1.8 million euros over four years to invest in Velsen.

216008.explanation: GroenLinks considers the dog tax outdated and wants to abolish it, as has happened in most municipalities. This way, everyone is free to own a dog. However, we do want proper enforcement of the obligation to clean up after dogs. This ensures a clean and livable municipality for everyone.

216009.explanation: GroenLinks believes extra funding for campaigns is unnecessary. Companies, like new residents, already like to establish themselves in Velsen. We prefer to invest in sustainable businesses and accessibility rather than advertising. At the same time, we want to preserve and enhance the green character of our residential areas.

216010.explanation: GroenLinks wants to accelerate the transition to cheaper green energy for all residents. That is why we want to support energy cooperatives and other initiatives through which residents can locally generate energy. This reduces pressure on the grid, increases control, and combats CO2 emissions.

216011.explanation: GroenLinks wants to continue giving priority for housing to vulnerable residents, such as victims of domestic violence and people with a residence permit. This helps them integrate faster and prevents overcrowded shelter locations. (In 2024, 39 of the 729 homes in Velsen were allocated to status holders.)

216012.explanation: GroenLinks finds it important that new residents of Velsen can participate well and quickly. Therefore, we want to collaborate with social organizations and invest in language lessons, guidance to work, and encounters with local residents, for example through a buddy project.

216013.explanation: GroenLinks stands for humane shelter for people fleeing war and violence. In Velsen, the location for 200 people in Santpoort-Zuid appears to be the most suitable. In consultation with neighbors, we ensure safety and facilities, along with a green plan, also for after the asylum seekers' center (AZC) closes after 10 years.

216016.explanation: GroenLinks believes residents should have more opportunities for say in their neighborhood. By collectively caring for trees, plants, a playground, or a community garden, we make neighborhoods more beautiful and healthier while strengthening contact among residents.

216017.explanation: GroenLinks believes everyone should have access to sports, both at school and in their free time. By reimbursing membership fees of sports clubs for people with limited financial means, we ensure everyone can participate and contribute to the health of all residents.

216020.explanation: GroenLinks sees community enforcement officers (boa’s) as vital eyes and ears in the neighborhood. We advocate for more boa’s and youth boa’s who closely collaborate with our neighborhood police officers, community connectors, youth workers, and residents. In this way, we enhance safety and approachability on the streets.

216021.explanation: GroenLinks opts for prevention and enforcement instead of increased camera surveillance. While cameras can sometimes enhance the feeling of security, they also infringe on privacy. Therefore, we only support temporary camera surveillance where enforcement is difficult and if its use is well justified and discussed.

216025.explanation: GroenLinks supports green and mixed neighborhoods, and more affordable housing. In Santpoort-Noord, Santpoort-Zuid, Velserbroek, Velsen-Zuid, and Driehuis, few social rental homes exist. We want to increase the percentage of social rental units in new construction projects there from 30 to 40 percent.

216026.explanation: GroenLinks supports green and mixed neighborhoods, and more affordable housing. In IJmuiden and Velsen-Noord there are already many social rental homes, so we especially want new mid-priced rental and owner-occupied housing there. Nevertheless, we want to continue enabling social rental housing, also due to demolition and new construction.

216027.explanation: GroenLinks believes houses are meant to be lived in, not to generate profit. We support a mandatory owner-occupancy requirement and an anti-speculation clause. This keeps homes available and affordable for our residents.

216028.explanation: GroenLinks wants more affordable homes. By converting vacant offices and shops into housing, we quickly create extra living space. The same applies to splitting houses and adding extra floors (roof extensions). This is faster than new construction and does not come at the expense of green spaces.

216030.explanation: GroenLinks wants to combat poverty, especially among children. We start in neighborhoods where poverty is above the national average: Zee- and Duinwijk and Velsen-Noord. Therefore, we want to invest, not cut, in poverty reduction, debt prevention, and a neighborhood-focused approach.

216031.explanation: GroenLinks wants to better support caregivers, especially young caregivers who often do not realize they provide care. We want to focus on peer contact and so-called respite care (temporary substitute care), as well as exemption from the obligation to apply for jobs.

216032.explanation: GroenLinks stands for equal opportunities for children and youth. That is why we want to invest in effective youth work, youth centers, and cooperation with education and care providers. Good guidance prevents mental problems and problematic behavior, offers young people perspective, and a safe place.

216033.explanation: GroenLinks wants a green living environment for everyone and to green the most paved neighborhoods. We prefer fewer cars on the street or green parking spaces (smart combinations). In consultation with residents, we will examine parking hubs at the edges of neighborhoods with high parking pressure.

216034.explanation: GroenLinks wants an accessible living environment for everyone. In neighborhoods with high parking pressure and/or where new homes are built, we focus on public transport. Additionally, we want to discuss permit parking there, as it may be necessary to keep neighborhoods accessible.

216035.explanation: GroenLinks wants a safe living environment for everyone. Too many accidents with pedestrians and cyclists occur in Velsen. A maximum speed of 30 km/h makes our roads demonstrably much safer for these vulnerable groups. On through-roads, a maximum speed of 50 km/h may apply.

216036.explanation: GroenLinks stands for a connected society in which people care for each other and can meet. Community centers, but also churches and associations, are important for social cohesion. Where such a facility is lacking and residents need it, we will explore the possibilities.

216038.explanation: GroenLinks wants the voices of residents up to 28 years old to be better heard. Therefore, we want to create policy not only for, but especially with our young people. We will increase youth participation and actively introduce young people to politics, with a youth council and a youth mayor.

216039.explanation: GroenLinks wants everyone to be able to easily travel from A to B, even if you do not have a car, have limited means, or are older. That is why we want to make a cheap bus subscription or free public transport possible for residents with low income and the elderly.

216040.explanation: GroenLinks puts health first. We will continue to pressure Tata Steel for clean air for us and our children. We want measurable health benefits now and the rapid closure of the most polluting parts of Tata Steel. We also want a plan B if the green steel plans do not proceed.

216044.explanation: GroenLinks invests in living arrangements where older people can remain independent longer with care nearby. Think of 'intergenerational living' (where multiple generations live at one address) or models where people live independently together and organize care jointly (such as shared housing communities).

216045.explanation: GroenLinks wants to plant 1,000 trees annually. Trees are good for health and help combat climate change. We also apply the 3-30-300 rule: from your window you can see at least 3 mature trees, at least 30 percent of outdoor air is covered with leaves, and there is a park within 300 meters.

216046.explanation: GroenLinks sees relationship therapy as preventive care. Residents with limited means should also have access to this. By providing timely help, we prevent (acrimonious) divorces and social and mental problems, also for children who can be victims of parental conflicts.

216048.explanation: GroenLinks wants to counteract illiteracy and promote reading. This requires a broad approach with schools, parents, and the library, an ideal meeting place. A helpful step is that the library card is already free until the age of 28. A logical next step is a free subscription for everyone.

216049.explanation: GroenLinks wants to better support caregivers, especially young caregivers who often do not realize they provide care. We want to focus on peer contact and so-called respite care (temporary substitute care).

216051.explanation: GroenLinks wants more affordable housing. By converting vacant offices and shops into housing, we quickly create extra living space. The same applies to splitting houses and adding extra floors (roof extensions). This is faster than new construction and does not come at the expense of green spaces.

216052.explanation: GroenLinks wants to continue helping homeowners insulate their homes. This lowers energy costs, increases living comfort, and is good for the climate. We also want to fully commit to insulating (social) rental properties and continue to combat energy poverty in the municipality.

216053.explanation: GroenLinks wants to improve Velsen's accessibility, especially on foot, by bike, and by public transport. The De Heerenduinen swimming pool is an important facility for everyone, especially children and the elderly. A bus stop there seems a good idea because it would make the pool more accessible.

216054.explanation: GroenLinks believes gambling and alcohol do not need to be promoted. The same applies to flying and the fossil fuel industry. The health of residents and the climate outweigh the interests and profits of companies. Therefore, we want to investigate a ban, following many other municipalities.

216055.explanation: GroenLinks wants to counteract the effects of climate change such as flooding and drought. Therefore, we create so-called wadi’s, decouple rainwater from the sewer, and make parking spaces with grass tiles. This allows water to slowly seep into the ground and collects water for nature during dry periods.

216056.explanation: GroenLinks wants a green living environment for everyone and to green the most paved neighborhoods. In places with many visitors, we can expand or introduce paid parking. The money raised from this we will use to stimulate sustainable alternatives to the car.

216057.explanation: GroenLinks finds the current recycling fee fairer, cheaper, and better for the climate because it encourages separation and reduces waste. Households with diapers should be able to receive an exemption. We also ensure strict enforcement against illegal dumping.

216058.explanation: GroenLinks wants to make trial cohabitation possible, following many other municipalities. Residents with social benefits can then live together for six months without financial consequences. A major advantage of this arrangement is that it prevents subletting, freeing up one of the homes after the trial period.

216059.explanation: GroenLinks believes no one should be left without income, including residents up to 28 years old. By eliminating the waiting period for young people to receive benefits, we can prevent youth from facing mental problems or homelessness without a safety net.

216061.explanation: GroenLinks wants to keep permits affordable for entrepreneurs. Especially permits for sustainable and local initiatives that benefit the climate or our residents can, in our view, be cheaper.

216062.explanation: GroenLinks wants to encourage companies to jointly create green, safe, and sustainable sites and buildings. This benefits employees, neighborhood residents, and the climate.

216063.explanation: GroenLinks believes that all sports facilities should be accessible to all residents of Velsen, because everyone should be able to participate in sports. In addition, we want to make all municipal buildings, amenities, playgrounds, exercise areas, and (digital) information well accessible to everyone.

216064.explanation: GroenLinks considers it important that new residents of Velsen can participate well and quickly. Therefore, we want to invest in language lessons for refugees, additional language education for children aged 2 to 6 who need it, and newcomer education.

216007.explanation: In the coming years, pressure on the municipal finances will be high. Due to cutbacks from the national government, we need the revenues from property tax (OZB) to keep social facilities such as the swimming pool, libraries, and community centers open.

216008.explanation: Dog owners contribute to the costs of keeping neighborhoods and green spaces clean.

216009.explanation: An attractive climate for starting entrepreneurs in Velsen is important but is primarily the responsibility of the business sector itself. Moreover, City Marketing Velsen is already doing great work in this area.

216010.explanation: Everyone should benefit from the energy transition. Therefore, we support residents who generate sustainable energy together. With energy coaches, we provide help, advice, and products for insulation measures and encourage initiatives that make neighborhoods and homes sustainably fair.

216011.explanation: The shortage of social rental housing in Velsen is significant. The solution is not to change priority rules but to build more. Priority rules also help to better distribute status holders and thus protect the most vulnerable neighborhoods.

216012.explanation: We want rapid integration through education, language lessons, and work, so everyone can participate immediately and acquire skills for the future.

216013.explanation: We support humane and sustainable shelter for people fleeing war and violence. The Elta location in Santpoort-Zuid is a good place for refugee reception: close to facilities, in a socially strong neighborhood, and it ensures a fair distribution across villages and neighborhoods.

216016.explanation: We support residents who commit to a clean, green neighborhood. A neighborhood concierge can help with this.

216017.explanation: Sports give energy and keep you healthy. We believe it is important that people with a low income can also participate in sports. Velsen has (minimum income) schemes for this. We want more households in Velsen to be able to use this income support.

216020.explanation: Everyone should feel safe in Velsen. We achieve this not only with enforcement officers but especially through the preventive efforts of neighborhood connectors and youth workers.

216021.explanation: We believe there should not be more camera surveillance just like that, especially not if it comes at the expense of privacy. In some situations, such as serious and persistent nuisance, camera surveillance is justified.

216025.explanation: In these village centers, we want 40% of new housing to be social rental housing.

216026.explanation: In IJmuiden and Velsen-Noord, the proportion of social rental housing is high. In these villages, we believe it is important for housing diversity and neighborhood livability that mainly affordable rental and owner-occupied homes in the middle segment are built.

216027.explanation: We believe affordable housing should be protected against purchase by investors. By requiring residents to live in the home themselves, we ensure houses remain available for starters and middle incomes. This gives them a better chance of affordable housing.

216028.explanation: In times of housing shortage, vacancy is unacceptable. By converting office buildings into homes, we strengthen livability and social cohesion. Active vacancy policy ensures vital, safe neighborhoods where everyone can find a home.

216030.explanation: We remain committed to providing everyone with economic security. We want to prevent people from getting into debt, give children growing up in poverty equal opportunities, and ensure low-income households make use of all the facilities they are entitled to.

216031.explanation: Intensive informal care can be demanding. Those who care for a loved one should not be obliged to apply for jobs. We want more trust and tailored support in social assistance, so municipalities can exempt caregivers from the job application requirement.

216032.explanation: Youth workers help children and young people grow up healthy and with opportunities. It helps prevent problems such as school dropout and unemployment. By investing in prevention, we avoid more serious issues later. This way, we give young people equal opportunities.

216033.explanation: We want a fair distribution of public space for green areas, water, and parking, tailored to each neighborhood and village. More parking spaces come at the expense of green spaces and safe streets. We choose livable neighborhoods and good public transport and bicycle facilities.

216034.explanation: We want to expand parking permits in neighborhoods where parking pressure is too high. This way, residents get space in their own street again, search traffic decreases, and livability improves. We will do this affordably and in consultation with the neighborhood.

216035.explanation: Reducing the maximum speed to 30 km/h in residential and shopping areas makes these areas safer for traffic. For cycling children and elderly people with limited mobility, this makes a world of difference.

216036.explanation: We want places in every neighborhood where people can meet, such as community centers and libraries, for social interaction and help with questions.

216038.explanation: We believe young people should have a voice in policies that affect them. A youth council helps us better understand what young people need. With a youth council, the municipality gets direct input from young people themselves, so policies better match their needs.

216039.explanation: We want public transport to be accessible and affordable for everyone. Therefore, we focus on stops within 800 meters of every home and on research into free public transport for low-income households and seniors.

216040.explanation: Tata Steel is an important employer in the IJmond region and must also take responsibility for a healthy living environment. Clean air is a priority. Therefore, accelerated closure of the most polluting installations may be necessary.

216044.explanation: We invest in small-scale housing forms, such as Knarrenhof, where elderly people can live together with care nearby. This prevents loneliness, relieves informal caregivers, and helps older adults remain independent longer. It is a sustainable solution to the growing demand for care.

216045.explanation: More trees in residential areas and village centers enhance livability and ensure cleaner and healthier air. Therefore, it is good if the municipality invests in additional greenery wherever possible.

216046.explanation: We advocate accessible care and support, especially for people with low incomes. In divorces, couple therapy can help prevent escalation and better support parents and children. This creates stable home situations and equal opportunities for all children.

216048.explanation: Low literacy excludes people. We fight for equal opportunities and therefore fully commit to language proficiency. Together with the library and partners, we continue to combat illiteracy. A free subscription for children, young people up to 28 years old, and low-income residents is essential in this.

216049.explanation: Informal caregivers often become overburdened while doing indispensable work. We want to better support them by investing in replacement care, so (young) caregivers receive the rest, appreciation, and support they deserve in time.

216051.explanation: In times of housing shortage, vacancy is unacceptable. By converting retail spaces into homes, we strengthen livability and social cohesion. Active vacancy policy ensures vital, safe neighborhoods where everyone can find a home.

216052.explanation: We want all homes to be made sustainable quickly. Therefore, insulation subsidies must remain, especially for people with low incomes. Through this, we guide and support residents. This prevents energy poverty, debt, high bills, and residents being cold in winter.

216053.explanation: Everyone should be able to move around easily – including Velsen residents with a small budget. Therefore, we focus on affordable, accessible, and future-proof public transport. Specifically, this means we want public transport available within a maximum of 800 meters from every home in Velsen.

216054.explanation: We want to protect people from harmful temptations. Advertisements for gambling and alcohol normalize risky behavior and increase inequality. Therefore, they should not remain in public spaces; public spaces must be safe and healthy.

216055.explanation: We want to invest in rainwater capture to make neighborhoods climate resilient. With water retention basins (wadis), underground storage, and disconnecting rainwater from the sewer system, we prevent flooding and heat stress, ensuring a livable, healthy neighborhood for everyone.

216056.explanation: Paid parking at shopping centers ensures better traffic flow so fewer parking spaces are needed to keep shops accessible. By introducing parking permits and paid parking in residential neighborhoods where parking pressure is too high, residents will regain space in their own street.

216057.explanation: We choose fair rates: those who produce less waste and separate better pay less. We take into account low incomes and medical waste, and aim to reduce residual waste for a cleaner, livable neighborhood.

216058.explanation: We believe that everyone has the right to security and equal opportunities. Trial cohabitation should be possible for six months without losing benefits, allowances, or housing. This way, people can build their relationship without risks, and there will be more flow in the housing market.

216059.explanation: We want young people in Velsen to be able to receive benefits immediately. The four-week waiting period increases stress and inequality, especially for youth without a safety net. The law allows room to eliminate this, so everyone quickly gains security.

216061.explanation: Making permits cheaper is undesirable. The municipality should actually tighten permits for companies that discharge and emit harmful substances. This protects health and the living environment and ensures supervision of responsible business practices.

216062.explanation: Community funds should go to public facilities, not subsidies for companies. Cooperation between companies is positive, but investments in their own domain remain primarily their own responsibility.

216063.explanation: We want an inclusive municipality where everyone can participate. Therefore, all sports facilities must be fully accessible. Sports clubs are the social glue of Velsen; accessibility ensures that people with disabilities can also participate and develop themselves.

216064.explanation: We invest in language lessons so that refugees can integrate faster. Out of solidarity, we share responsibility together. Early integration through language, school, and work prevents disadvantages and helps newcomers become full members of our society.

216007.explanation: Sterk Velsen believes that the property tax (OZB) should remain stable except for inflation correction.

216008.explanation: Agree, because the revenues are no longer used as a goal to combat nuisance.

216009.explanation: Currently, the municipality already has city marketing which is funded by the municipality. That is sufficient.

216010.explanation: This should NOT be regulated locally but nationally.

216011.explanation: Sterk Velsen is of the opinion that they, like every resident in Velsen, should join the waiting list; besides, municipalities nationwide already have targets.

216012.explanation: According to Sterk Velsen, this should be arranged nationally.

216013.explanation: Sterk Velsen has suggested a more suitable location in the rural area.

216016.explanation: Sterk Velsen believes this is a task of the municipality.

216017.explanation: Provisions for this have already been arranged here.

216020.explanation: Safety is paramount to Sterk Velsen.

216021.explanation: Safety is paramount to Sterk Velsen.

216025.explanation: Vulnerable neighborhoods should be relieved in this matter.

216026.explanation: Vulnerable neighborhoods should be relieved in this matter.

216027.explanation: Except in the case of possible purchase by parents or children.

216028.explanation: Agree, but not that it must be done, rather allowing private individuals to do this as well. After all, the municipality does not always control what happens to an office building if it is not the owner.

216030.explanation: Everyone should be able to participate.

216031.explanation: Agree, Sterk Velsen believes strict control should be exercised to verify that an official informal care agreement is present.

216032.explanation: Sterk Velsen believes youth work plays an important role, for example, in being able to act preventively.

216033.explanation: There are too few parking spaces in the neighborhoods. Sterk Velsen believes that more parking spaces can also go hand in hand with more greenery in the neighborhoods.

216035.explanation: Disagree, but Sterk Velsen does believe that within existing 30 km/h zones this should be more clearly and frequently indicated and that physical adjustments should make speeding less attractive.

216036.explanation: Every Velsen resident has the right to accessible culture.

216038.explanation: Sterk Velsen values the voice of youth and is itself the initiator of this proposal.

216040.explanation: The environmental service already controls this.

216044.explanation: Sterk Velsen believes this should be arranged nationally and favors reopening nursing homes.

216045.explanation: Sterk Velsen supports having not more, but sufficient trees in the neighborhoods.

216049.explanation: Measures are already in place for this: for example, the Health Insurance Act and respite care.

216055.explanation: The municipality already provides for this now.

216057.explanation: Recycling pays off, but Sterk Velsen supports sorting waste after collection if this is financially advantageous for our residents.

216058.explanation: It is indirectly already arranged in the Participation Act; requesting permission in advance is also more controllable.

216061.explanation: Sterk Velsen supports a more flexible establishment policy.

216064.explanation: Status holders who are allowed to remain in the Netherlands also have the right to work in the Netherlands and the possibility to afford language lessons.

216007.explanation: The CDA wants to keep municipal taxes as low as possible, but always with an eye on a healthy budget and strong facilities. If cutbacks weigh too heavily on society or threaten the municipality’s financial foundation, a property tax increase may be necessary as a last resort.

216008.explanation: The money generated by the dog tax was originally intended to be spent only on facilities for dog owners and to cover costs caused by dogs; that is no longer the case. Moreover, the municipality incurs many administrative costs to collect this tax.

216009.explanation: Promotion makes Velsen attractive and thereby stimulates the local economy. In the eyes of the CDA, city marketing has delivered good results in recent years. This money is therefore well spent socially.

216010.explanation: Local energy initiatives increase support and involvement. The municipality should primarily facilitate this with knowledge and guidance. The CDA is also in favor of (limited) financial support when there is significant societal added value.

216011.explanation: The municipality has a legal duty to house status holders. This is necessary to keep the asylum chain functioning and to enable rapid integration of status holders. For strong and mixed neighborhoods, the CDA chooses not to house status holders in IJmuiden and Velsen-Noord.

216012.explanation: Rapid integration through language, work, and participation in society prevents problems in the long term and is therefore a good investment in our Velsen community. We are currently spending enough on this. Additional funding is not needed.

216013.explanation: Municipalities have a legal task to accommodate asylum seekers; otherwise, the asylum chain stalls. People applying for asylum here must be properly housed. Studies show that an asylum seekers' center (AZC) for 200 asylum seekers in Santpoort-Zuid is appropriate, with livability and safety as conditions.

216016.explanation: The CDA believes it is important that the municipality encourages people to maintain their own neighborhood. Well-maintained neighborhoods support mutual involvement and livability.

216017.explanation: Sport contributes to health, social interaction, and participation in society. Those who cannot afford to play sports should not be left on the sidelines. Therefore, the municipality assists if necessary through so-called minimum support schemes. The CDA wants to simplify the complexity of these support schemes.

216020.explanation: The CDA primarily wants to focus on prevention, early detection, and strengthening strong communities. Investing in strong neighborhoods where people know and address each other works better than only deploying enforcement officers. Nevertheless, enforcement officers play an extremely important role in the feeling of safety.

216021.explanation: The CDA believes cameras can be effective in certain locations to improve safety, but only where demonstrably necessary. Focusing on prevention and neighborhoods with strong social cohesion should take precedence.

216025.explanation: The CDA wants to focus on strong neighborhoods. A mix of social rental and owner-occupied homes creates more resilience in neighborhoods, and distribution is necessary for this. Therefore, we consciously choose to only build social rental homes in these residential cores. For projects of more than 10 homes, a maximum of 30% social housing applies.

216026.explanation: The CDA wants to focus on strong neighborhoods. A mix of social rental and owner-occupied homes creates more resilience in neighborhoods, and distribution is necessary for this. Therefore, we consciously choose to only build social rental homes in these residential cores. For projects of more than 10 homes, a maximum of 30% social housing applies.

216027.explanation: We want homes to be a place to call home, not a source of profit. Therefore, we promote owner-occupation where legally and practically possible.

216028.explanation: The CDA wants to utilize vacancy and encourage mixed uses (working, living, culture) to keep shopping streets and neighborhoods vital. Making office buildings suitable for residential use is not simple but can provide relief in the overheated housing market.

216030.explanation: Fighting poverty is a core task. Residents living in poverty face many hardships across various areas. Our poverty policy and making minimum support schemes more accessible (and even proactively offering them) ensures that more residents can participate in society.

216031.explanation: Informal care is highly valuable for our society. As far as the CDA is concerned, informal care can be a reason for (temporary) exemption from the job application requirement in social assistance, but this is always done on a case-by-case basis.

216032.explanation: Youth work helps us connect with and gain the trust of our young people. It is a strong method of prevention and delivering care where needed. CDA Velsen wants to invest specifically in youth work, meeting places, and prevention to strengthen social cohesion and safety.

216033.explanation: The growth of our municipality puts pressure on space. The CDA therefore chooses livable neighborhoods with sufficient space for greenery, water, and social encounters. This sometimes requires different parking choices: not always directly at the front door, but nearby, safe, and tailored to the needs of each neighborhood.

216034.explanation: The CDA is neither for nor against parking permits per se. In some neighborhoods, they might be necessary to fairly allocate scarce space, but they are not an end in themselves and must always be carefully and neighborhood-focused assessed.

216035.explanation: 30 km/h improves traffic safety and livability. CDA Velsen wants safe and livable neighborhoods and encourages walking and cycling. A lower speed fits this vision of traffic safety and livability. However, CDA chooses to retain 50 km/h on main traffic arteries to ensure flow.

216036.explanation: Meeting places are important for connectedness in neighborhoods. They help people get to know each other and see how everyone is doing. Not every neighborhood needs a community center; the need and existing places determine this per neighborhood. The initiative lies with residents, and the municipality facilitates.

216038.explanation: By involving young people early in politics, we strengthen their sense of responsibility and ownership over their living environment. The CDA finds this important. The CDA is open to contributions to youth engagement through a youth council, but this is not the only way.

216039.explanation: The CDA focuses on fighting poverty by merging current minimum support schemes and making them more accessible. This makes poverty more bearable and allows residents to decide how to spend the money, without creating specific public transport schemes.

216040.explanation: Health and living environment require reducing harmful emissions, while also keeping an eye on employment and future prospects. The CDA advocates accelerated replacement of outdated installations as part of a realistic sustainability trajectory.

216044.explanation: The CDA wants seniors to live independently as long as possible, with safety and care nearby. Housing forms that combine living, care, and social encounters are valuable. The municipality facilitates through cooperation, space, and conditions but does not structurally finance commercial housing care initiatives.

216045.explanation: Greenery is highly valuable for livability and climate: trees provide cooling, water retention, and pleasant living. The CDA wants to design neighborhoods to be green and climate-resistant, with attention to differences between neighborhoods. It's not about numbers but the effect; tailor-made solutions per neighborhood and village.

216046.explanation: Good relationships are important for children, but the government cannot cover all therapy costs. The CDA does want to intervene in escalating divorces to prevent harm to children; this comes from the youth care budgets.

216048.explanation: Libraries are important, but making them free for everyone requires financial considerations. Targeted accessibility is preferred (whom do you want to encourage to use the library more), as many residents of Velsen can naturally afford the library themselves.

216049.explanation: Overburdening must be prevented. Respite care is crucial.

216051.explanation: In some cases, certainly. Redevelopment can help with structural vacancy, but the CDA prefers tailor-made approaches and not automatic conversion of shops into homes.

216052.explanation: It is important that the municipality remains involved in making residents' homes sustainable. This ensures more comfort, lower energy bills, and progress toward achieving our climate goals.

216053.explanation: Good accessibility of facilities is important, especially for less mobile residents. It is important, however, that the need for this is clear and significant.

216054.explanation: The CDA does not want to fuel addiction; therefore, we do not want gambling and alcohol use to be promoted in public spaces.

216055.explanation: Due to climate change, we face heavy rainfall more frequently. It is therefore wise to pay more attention to capturing and retaining rainwater during the redesign of neighborhoods and villages. This requires targeted investments, especially where water nuisance occurs.

216056.explanation: Only if necessary. Tailor-made solutions and support are leading.

216057.explanation: Separating waste must pay off. Differentiation stimulates sustainability.

216058.explanation: The CDA believes rules should not hinder people from living together and taking responsibility. At the same time, social assistance is regulated nationally, and the municipality cannot simply allow cohabitation without consequences. We advocate for more tailor-made approaches, clear rules, and room to guide people.

216059.explanation: The CDA finds it important to encourage young people to work or study, but rules must not lead to problems or debt. The waiting period is set nationally, and we only want the municipality to deviate from it in specific situations.

216061.explanation: Costs must be reasonable, but service and thoroughness remain important.

216062.explanation: The CDA supports entrepreneurs who invest together in their environment but believes that municipal contributions should always be accompanied by their own effort and clear public added value.

216063.explanation: For the CDA, participation is essential because people only thrive in connection with others and thus contribute to a strong community. This means removing barriers for people with disabilities, including at sports facilities.

216064.explanation: The municipality already has the legal task to organize language lessons for status holders. The CDA finds it important that these lessons are of good quality and lead to participation in society. Additional funds are only warranted if demonstrably necessary to achieve this goal.

216007.explanation: The property tax (OZB) is a structural cost increase for residents and entrepreneurs and undermines affordability and the business climate. The municipality should first save on bureaucracy, external hiring, and very expensive left-wing hobbies before asking citizens for more money.

216008.explanation: The dog tax is an outdated and unfair levy that bears no relation to actual costs. The municipality should use general funds for public spaces, not impose additional charges on specific groups.

216009.explanation: Velsen becomes attractive through low taxes, fast permit issuance, and reliable infrastructure, not through costly promotional campaigns. Municipal money should go towards basic conditions, not marketing talk and consultants. The municipality must focus more on its core tasks.

216010.explanation: Sustainable energy initiatives must be financially viable on their own and not dependent on municipal subsidies. Public money should not be used for energy experiments with uncertain benefits and high costs to the community.

216011.explanation: Social rental homes are scarce and must primarily be available to residents of Velsen who live, work here, and have often waited for years. Priority for status holders is unfair and undermines support for the social system.

216012.explanation: More money for integration prolongs a failing migration model and increases dependence on the government. Municipal resources should be used for remigration and relieving facilities for the local community.

216013.explanation: Forum for Democracy is the only party in Velsen that consistently and principledly opposes the establishment of an asylum seekers' center. An asylum seekers' center in Santpoort-Zuid harms livability, safety, and community support and must not be forced against the will of residents.

216016.explanation: Maintenance of public spaces is a core municipal task. Structural funding of residents' initiatives leads to inequality, arbitrariness between neighborhoods, and shirking of responsibilities.

216017.explanation: Fully reimbursing sports memberships for welfare recipients increases the imbalance between working and non-working individuals. Work must pay off, and municipal policy should not further undermine the incentive to work. The working residents of Velsen have to pay for these perks.

216020.explanation: Visible enforcement increases safety and restores authority in public spaces. Additional special enforcement officers are justified when they focus on nuisance, order, and livability, not on symbolic enforcement.

216021.explanation: Targeted and temporary camera surveillance at problem locations enhances safety and works preventively. We reject permanent surveillance; deployment must always be proportional and temporary, under the control of the municipal council.

216025.explanation: New social rental homes should be distributed evenly throughout the municipality. A fair balance prevents overburdening specific neighborhoods and protects livability and support.

216026.explanation: IJmuiden and Velsen-Noord already bear a disproportionate share of the social housing stock. Further concentration undermines livability and calls for a fairer distribution within the municipality.

216027.explanation: Homes are meant to be lived in, not for speculation. A mandatory owner-occupancy requirement protects first-time buyers and families and prevents the local housing market from being bought up by investors.

216028.explanation: Converting vacant office buildings into homes is logical and prevents unnecessary new construction. Urbanizing parts of the port area can be considered, provided it does not come at the expense of business activity, employment, and the economic function of the port.

216030.explanation: Poverty policy has gone too far into fragmented schemes and project subsidies without demonstrable effect. The municipality must focus on work, simplicity, and self-reliance rather than an expanding safety net that maintains dependency.

216031.explanation: Informal caregiving deserves appreciation and support, but a general exemption from the job application requirement undermines the principle that work and participation are leading. Tailored approaches remain necessary.

216032.explanation: Youth work must be cleansed of ideological projects and external organizations without demonstrable effect. Only practical, local, and result-oriented youth work that contributes to order, structure, and guidance deserves support.

216033.explanation: Additional parking spaces are needed to reduce nuisance and search traffic in residential neighborhoods. Accessibility and livability must not be sacrificed to Velsen's current ideological anti-parking policy.

216034.explanation: Parking permits restrict residents' freedom and shift the problem to other neighborhoods. The municipality should focus on providing sufficient parking spaces instead of new rules and burdens.

216035.explanation: A general reduction to 30 km/h is petty paternalism that harms accessibility. Traffic policy should be based on actual safety problems, not ideological behavior control.

216036.explanation: A mandatory community center in every neighborhood leads to vacancy and unnecessary costs. Facilities must meet actual needs and support, not be imposed as a policy goal.

216038.explanation: Democracy is an active process and requires active involvement of ALL residents, including explicitly young people. By seriously involving them in decision-making, we strengthen support, responsibility, and trust in local government.

216039.explanation: WORK MUST REMAIN REWARDING! Free or subsidized public transport for specific income groups increases dependency and shifts costs to working residents. The municipality should focus on affordability for everyone, not on new income-dependent schemes.

216040.explanation: Tata Steel is the backbone of the local economy, and closing installations threatens jobs and livelihoods. Environmental and health improvements must come through realistic modernization and investments, not through accelerated closure under political pressure.

216044.explanation: Allowing elderly people to live independently longer with care nearby prevents expensive nursing home admissions and heavy social support trajectories. This reduces structural care costs and promotes housing market flow, making the investment self-financing.

216045.explanation: More trees can contribute to livability and comfort, provided this occurs within existing budgets and through regular maintenance, without costly expansion programs.

216046.explanation: Although stable and loving relationships are of great value to society, couples therapy is not a core municipal task. Scarce resources must be used for care, safety, and security, not for individual therapy forms.

216048.explanation: The library is important, but a free subscription for everyone is unselective and costly. Targeted support is preferred.

216049.explanation: Targeted respite care prevents overload of young informal caregivers and heavier care later. The condition is that the support remains temporary, verifiable, and efficient.

216051.explanation: Converting vacant retail premises into housing addresses vacancy and increases housing supply without additional land use. This strengthens livability and can be faster and cheaper than large-scale new construction.

216052.explanation: Structural subsidies for home insulation put the cost on the community and mainly benefit those who can already invest. The municipality should focus on affordability and freedom of choice, not on permanent subsidization of private property.

216053.explanation: An additional bus stop at De Heerenduinen improves accessibility for young people, elderly, and families. It is a targeted, practical measure that increases mobility without large costs or ideological policy.

216054.explanation: Banning advertising is paternalism and shifts moral choices from citizens to the government. The municipality must respect freedom and limit itself to enforcement of existing legislation.

216055.explanation: Targeted investments in rainwater collection prevent flooding and damage during heavy showers. It must be done frugally and efficiently, linked to regular maintenance and without costly climate projects.

216056.explanation: More paid parking is nothing but a disguised tax increase for residents and entrepreneurs. It reduces accessibility, drives people away from shopping areas, and does not solve parking problems but merely shifts them.

216057.explanation: Equal rates are fairer and prevent petty behavioral control via waste policy. The municipality must ensure good collection, not financially punish residents for their residual waste.
